questions.question: Which of the following quiz question types is most likely to create accessibility barriers for students using keyboard navigation or screen readers?

options.text: Multiple choice with clearly labeled radio buttons

questions.explanation: Drag-and-drop matching requires users to interact via mouse-based dragging, which has no keyboard-accessible alternative. This violates WCAG 2.2 criterion 2.5.7 (Dragging Movements). Multiple choice, true/false, and short answer questions are all keyboard-accessible if properly implemented. The accessible alternative to drag-and-drop matching is to use separate multiple-choice questions — one for each term to be matched.

questions.question: How can a faculty member quickly test whether their quiz is keyboard-accessible before publishing it?

options.text: Ask a colleague to review it for accessibility
Run the Microsoft Accessibility Checker on the quiz file
Try completing the entire quiz using only Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, and arrow keys — no mouse
Check the LMS settings to see if accessibility is enabled

questions.explanation: The most direct way to test keyboard accessibility is to experience the quiz as a keyboard-only user would. This 2-minute test reveals immediately whether focus is visible, Tab order works, and all question types can be answered without a mouse. If you get stuck during the test, your students using keyboard navigation will too. While colleague feedback and checking LMS settings are valuable, only hands-on testing reveals the actual user experience.
